title: Claude in Slack
subtitle: Claude Tag: an assistant right inside your team's channels and direct messages

sources.title: What is Claude Tag — Claude Help Center
Claude in Slack — Claude Help Center

theory.text: Claude comes to where you work

theory.md:
Up until this world, you went to Claude yourself: you opened claude.ai, wrote a prompt, took the result. World 8 flips the model — now Claude is embedded into the tools where your team already spends the day: Slack, Excel, the browser. We'll start with Slack, because that's where the main raw material for an assistant lives — work discussions.

Think about how much value sinks into a messenger: decisions get made in fifty-message threads, answers to newcomers' eternal questions are scattered across channels, and 'does anyone remember what we decided back in March?' is a routine phrase. Claude in Slack turns this mountain of correspondence from a problem into a source of answers.

**Claude Tag** is Claude inside Slack. Once a workspace administrator connects it, team members can reach Claude in two main ways:

- **A mention in a channel or thread.** You write a message mentioning Claude — just as you'd call over a colleague — and it replies right in the thread, seeing the context of the discussion. This is the key superpower: you don't need to copy the conversation into a separate chat, Claude is already 'standing right there' reading the same thread.
- **Direct messages.** You can chat with Claude one on one, like with a regular team member: drafts, questions, breakdowns — anything you'd rather not bring into a shared channel.

It's important to understand the access model: Claude doesn't eavesdrop on all of Slack. It works with the context it was invited into — within the permissions configured at connection time. Invited into a thread — it sees the thread. Not invited — it doesn't take part.

rows: In a 60-message thread you mention Claude: 'give me a recap: what we decided, who's doing what'
20 minutes of reading for everyone who joins late
'What does our policy say about unpaid leave?' — Claude answers from the connected materials
A trip to HR and a search through the wiki
'Draft a polite reply to the client: the deadline slips by a week, the reason is their own extra edits'
Half an hour of agonizing over the wording
A newcomer asks a question in the channel — Claude gives the first answer, the team fills in the rest
Senior engineers' time on repetitive questions
In the marketing channel: 'suggest 10 name options for the newsletter, tone — friendly'
A blank page and silence in the channel

theory.text: How to phrase requests in threads

theory.md:
Everything you learned about prompts in World 1 works here too — with one adjustment: **Claude already sees the thread's context**, so don't retell the conversation. Instead, tell it what to do with it: 'pull out the decisions and open questions', 'phrase a summary for the director in three points', 'suggest a compromise between Sasha's and Lena's options'.

And remember that this happens in a team space: Claude's reply in a channel is seen by everyone in it. Contentious drafts and personal questions are better worked out in direct messages with Claude, bringing only the finished result into the channel.

theory.text: How to help Claude take root in a team

theory.md:
Technically, connecting is the administrator's job, but culturally Claude takes root through habits, and anyone can play the pioneer's role here. What works in practice:

- **Start with yourself and visible wins.** A couple of public summaries of long threads — and colleagues will ask you 'how did you do that?' themselves.
- **Agree on rituals.** For example: every thread longer than twenty messages is closed with a Claude summary; every newcomer's question first gets a Claude answer, then a clarification from the seniors.
- **Define the boundaries.** Teams with sensitive data should talk through in advance which channels and topics are off-limits for the assistant: this dispels skeptics' anxiety faster than any persuasion.
- **Don't replace people where a person is needed.** Conflict, feedback on work, personal matters — Claude can help with the wording in DMs, but the person should do the talking.

theory.bad: In a thread: '@Claude help'. Claude asks what exactly, the thread grows by another ten messages, colleagues watching.
theory.good: In a thread: '@Claude, summarize the discussion: 1) what we decided about the release date, 2) which risks were named, 3) who took which tasks. Format — a short list, so I can paste it into the final message'.
theory.explanation: Claude sees the thread but not your goal. 'Help' makes it guess in front of the whole team. A precise task with a format gives a ready result on the first reply — and looks professional in a shared channel.

theory.md: The Help Center (articles about Claude Tag in the Claude in Your Workspace section) describes Claude in Slack like this: you can interact with it through mentions in channels and threads and through direct messages; it helps summarize discussions, answer questions, and prepare drafts. Connecting is done by the workspace administrator, and Claude's access to data is defined by the connection settings.

Slack is a shared space. Before asking Claude to break down a thread with sensitive data (salaries, clients' personal data, conflicts), remember: both your request and the reply will stay in the channel in plain view of everyone. For anything delicate — direct messages, and better yet, check against company policy.

The best first habit: end long thread discussions with a request to Claude for a wrap-up — 'what we decided, who's doing it, by when'. In a month the team will have an archive of decisions instead of an archive of chatter.

tasks.question: What is Claude Tag?
options: A separate messenger app from Anthropic that replaces Slack
Claude inside Slack: you can mention it in channels and threads and write to it in direct messages
A tag in HTML markup for inserting Claude's answers into a website
A paid feature for exporting conversations from Slack into claude.ai
tasks.explanation: Claude Tag is Claude embedded into Slack. It answers where it's invited: in a thread by mention or in a direct message. Nothing needs to be replaced or exported anywhere.
tasks.hint: Recall the two ways to reach Claude in Slack from the theory.

tasks.question: You mentioned Claude in a thread in a work channel. What's important to remember about context and visibility? Select all that apply.
options: Claude sees the context of the thread it was invited into — there's no need to retell the conversation
Claude's reply in the channel is seen by everyone in it
By default Claude reads absolutely all Slack channels, even private ones
For delicate questions it's better to use direct messages with Claude
tasks.explanation: Claude works within the context it was invited into and the configured permissions — it doesn't 'eavesdrop on all of Slack'. But everything it replies in a channel is public to the members: anything delicate goes in DMs.
tasks.hint: One option contradicts the access model 'invited — sees it, not invited — doesn't take part'.

tasks.instruction: Slack simulation: a 47-message thread — the team argued about postponing the release. Mention Claude and give it a task about this thread.

steps.claudeReply:
Thread recap: 📋

**Decided:** the release moves from the 12th to the 19th — because of an unclosed bug in payments.

**Open questions:** who tells the clients (candidates — Marina or the account managers); whether a hotfix is needed for the current version.

**Tasks:** Dima — fix the bug by the 15th, Olya — regression test on the 16th–17th.

I can format this as an announcement for the general channel — want me to?
steps.failHint: Ask Claude for a recap of the thread: what was decided, which questions remain, who's doing what.

steps.claudeReply:
Draft announcement: 📣

> **The release is being postponed: 12th → 19th.**
> Reason: we're finishing work on payments (a bug was found in testing).
> Plan: fix by the 15th (Dima), regression on the 16th–17th (Olya), release on the 19th.
> Question for @Marina: are you taking the client communication?

Check the names and dates before publishing — then it's ready to send.
steps.failHint: Agree: 'Yes, format the announcement for the channel'.

tasks.successMessage: You've gone through the classic Claude-in-Slack cycle: thread → recap → a ready artifact for the team. Notice: the final word (checking and publishing) stays with the human.

tasks.instruction: A client in a shared channel is unhappy about the schedule slipping. Assemble a request to Claude for a draft reply — in the order: context → task → tone → format.
blocks: Context: the client is upset about the project delivery slipping by a week; the reason for the delay is their own extra edits, but that can't be said bluntly.
Task: draft a reply to the client acknowledging the inconvenience, explaining the reason, and giving the new date.
Tone: calm, respectful, without excuses or bureaucratic jargon.
Format: 4–6 sentences, ending with a concrete next step for the client.
distractors: Tell the client it's their own fault the schedule slipped.
Make the reply longer and weightier, about two pages.
tasks.explanation: The formula from World 1 works in Slack too: context → task → tone → format. Blaming the client will kill the relationship, and 'weightier, two pages' is a sure way to make sure nobody reads the letter to the end.

tasks.instruction:
**A mission in real Slack.**

⚠️ Required: a Slack workspace where an administrator has connected Claude (Claude Tag). If there isn't one — suggest the administrator read the 'What is Claude Tag' article from the lesson's sources, and do this mission later.

1. Find a thread with a discussion of 15+ messages (or a DM conversation with a large volume).
2. Mention Claude in the thread and ask for a recap: decisions, open questions, tasks.
3. Check the recap against the thread itself: is everything correct, is anything made up?
4. Separately, message Claude in direct messages and ask for a draft of any work message (an announcement, a reply to a colleague, a reminder).
5. Edit the draft to fit yourself before sending.
checklist: Claude replied in the thread on mention
The recap was checked against the real content of the thread
The draft from direct messages was edited, not sent blindly
